confirmed.title: Dead section-header rendering path: no JiraIssue with a "header-" id is ever created
confirmed.description: TicketListCellRenderer.isHeader() / paintSectionHeader() and SprintDashboardPanel.isHeader() implement an assignee-grouping feature (section header rows) that was never wired up. A repo-wide grep confirms no code anywhere constructs a JiraIssue whose id starts with "header-" — the "All Tickets" toggle (ToggleAllUsersAction) just re-fetches a flat list with allUsers=true, it never injects header rows. Consequently the entire paintSectionHeader() method (TicketListCellRenderer.kt:57-99), the HEADER_LINE_COLOR constant (line 282, used only there), and the positive branch of every isHeader() check are unreachable. The isHeader() guards in SprintDashboardPanel (lines 489, 930, 1222) always return false, so they are defensive code for an impossible state.
confirmed.evidence:
private fun isHeader(issue: JiraIssue): Boolean = issue.id.startsWith("header-")  // never matches — no "header-" issue is ever produced
if (isHeader(issue)) { paintSectionHeader(g, issue); return }
confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete paintSectionHeader() and HEADER_LINE_COLOR from TicketListCellRenderer, remove both isHeader() helpers, and drop the `&& !isHeader(selected)` / `if (isHeader(...)) return` guards in SprintDashboardPanel (lines 489, 930, 1222) and the renderer's paintComponent header dispatch (lines 105-108). If assignee grouping is a desired future feature, track it as a backlog item rather than keeping the half-built unreachable path.
confirmed.verdictReasoning:
I attempted to refute this finding but independently confirmed it from the code and git history.

REACHABILITY (confirmed dead): A repo-wide grep for the literal `header-` shows it appears ONLY in the two `isHeader()` definitions: TicketListCellRenderer.kt:55 (`issue.id.startsWith("header-")`) and SprintDashboardPanel.kt:205. No code anywhere constructs a `JiraIssue` whose id starts with `header-`. The list is populated solely via `updateList(issues)` (SprintDashboardPanel.kt:780-800), a flat loop `for (issue in sorted) { listModel.addElement(issue) }` with no grouping or synthetic-row injection. Every data source feeding `updateList` is `result.data` from SprintService (lines 588-589, 645-646, 727-728, 749-751) plus the search filter at line 872 — all raw `List<JiraIssue>` from the Jira API. Jira issue ids are numeric strings, so `startsWith("header-")` can never match. The `ToggleAllUsersAction` (lines 898-921) only flips `showAllUsers` and calls `loadData()`; it injects nothing (its description string "grouped by assignee" at line 915 is itself stale).

GIT HISTORY (proves it is leftover, not future scaffolding): The header path was introduced in commit 9c9ff2bf6 ("feat(sprint): add all-tickets view with assignee grouping"), which DID wire it up — it built `JiraIssue(id = "header-$assignee", ...)` and added it to the list model. It was then DELIBERATELY removed in commit 227284188 ("fix: sprint tab UI refinements per user feedback", explicit item 5: "Remove group headers (user name + ticket count above tickets)") per user request. That commit deleted the grouping/header-injection block in `updateList` but left behind the now-orphaned rendering scaffolding: `paintSectionHeader()` (TicketListCellRenderer.kt:57-99), `HEADER_LINE_COLOR` (line 282, used only by paintSectionHeader), both `isHeader()` helpers, the `paintComponent` dispatch (lines 105-108), and the guards at SprintDashboardPanel.kt:489, 930, 1222. So this is confirmed dead code, not a planned feature stub.

HARM OF FIX (none): The three guards all gate on `isHeader(selected)` which is permanently false: line 489 (selection listener) always takes the `detailPanel.showIssue(selected)` branch for any non-null selection; line 930 (`if (isHeader(selectedIssue)) return` in StartWorkAction) never early-returns; line 1222 (`isEnabled = selected != null && !isHeader(selected)`) reduces to `selected != null`. Removing the guards and the rendering path changes no observable behavior. No test exercises the header path — the only test touching the renderer (HtmlEscapeInTicketLabelsTest) covers tooltip HTML escaping (line 40 comment), not headers. So the fix breaks no passing test and removes no load-bearing fallback.

SEVERITY: The finder claimed Medium; I reassess to Low. This is purely a maintainability/dead-code issue — ~45 lines of unreachable rendering logic plus three no-op defensive guards. There is no user-facing wrong behavior, no crash, no perf cost (the dispatch is one always-false branch per paint), and no security impact. It is a clean deletion candidate but low priority.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Accept as a low-priority cleanup. Safe to delete paintSectionHeader() and HEADER_LINE_COLOR from TicketListCellRenderer.kt, remove both isHeader() helpers, and drop the now-trivial guards at SprintDashboardPanel.kt:489/930/1222 plus the paintComponent header dispatch (lines 105-108). Git history (227284188) confirms group headers were intentionally removed per user feedback, so there is no future-feature reason to retain the scaffolding; if assignee grouping is ever revived, track it as a backlog item. No tests guard this path, so no test changes are needed beyond a clean compile.

confirmed.title: Redundant no-op conditional: finalBranchClient if/else both return branchClient
confirmed.description: The `finalBranchClient` if/else evaluates `dialogResult.selectedRepoIndex != detectedIndex` but both branches return the identical `branchClient` value. The inline comment even states a new client "isn't needed". This is a no-op conditional that adds reader confusion (it implies branching behavior that does not exist).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Replace the if/else with `val finalBranchClient = branchClient`, or inline `branchClient` directly into the startWork(...) call at line 1188 and delete the local. The correct project/repo are already passed via finalProjectKey/finalRepoSlug.
confirmed.verdictReasoning:
I attempted to refute this but the code independently confirms it. At SprintDashboardPanel.kt:1150-1154 the if/else `val finalBranchClient = if (dialogResult.selectedRepoIndex != detectedIndex) { branchClient } else branchClient` returns the identical `branchClient` reference in both branches — no side effects, no logging, no different expression. The inline comment at line 1151-1153 even states a new client "isn't needed."

Why this is genuinely a no-op (architectural confirmation): `branchClient` is a single instance created once at line 984 via `BitbucketBranchClient.fromConfiguredSettings()`. Its constructor (core/.../BitbucketBranchClient.kt:778) takes ONLY `baseUrl`, `tokenProvider`, `timeouts` — it is NOT bound to any project/repo. Every method (`getBranches` line 978, `createBranch` line 1102, etc.) takes `projectKey`/`repoSlug` as per-call parameters. So the same client legitimately serves any repo. The same single `branchClient` is already reused at line 996 (initial fetch) and line 1044 (re-fetch on repo change). Repo selection is correctly threaded via `finalProjectKey`/`finalRepoSlug` (lines 1148-1149, derived from `selectedRepo` at line 1147), which are passed to startWork at lines 1189-1190. The conditional contributes nothing to that.

Not harmful to fix: collapsing to `val finalBranchClient = branchClient` (or inlining `branchClient` into the startWork call) is strictly behavior-preserving — the client is repo-agnostic, so both current branches already produce the same value. No test pins this conditional: the only test reference (StartWorkResultTest.kt:15) asserts `selectedRepoIndex` on the result DTO, unrelated to `finalBranchClient`. There is no load-bearing fallback being removed.

Severity: Low is accurate (pure readability/maintainability; the dead branch falsely implies branching behavior). Not a correctness, threading, security, or architecture issue.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Apply the suggested fix: replace lines 1150-1154 with `val finalBranchClient = branchClient`, or inline `branchClient` directly into the startWork(...) call at line 1188 and delete the local plus its misleading comment. Correct repo targeting is preserved by finalProjectKey/finalRepoSlug. Low priority style cleanup.

confirmed.title: Redundant duplicate settings write in TicketDetectionPresenter.onAccept
confirmed.description: onAccept writes settings.state.activeTicketId / activeTicketSummary directly and then immediately calls ActiveTicketService.setActiveTicket(...). Per the Phase 5 T13 facade design (ActiveTicketService docs) the canonical write goes through WorkflowContextService.setActiveTicket, which itself writes those exact same settings fields (WorkflowContextService.kt:341-342). The sibling accept path in SprintDashboardPanel was already cleaned up (line 266-268 comment: 'facade now persists via WorkflowContextService ... so the inline settings writes + EventBus emit are gone'); this presenter still carries the duplicate write.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Drop the two inline `settings.state.activeTicket*` assignments and keep only the setActiveTicket(...) call, mirroring the already-cleaned SprintDashboardPanel.onAccept path. (Note: setActiveTicket dispatches the canonical persist on a background scope; if a synchronous settings value is required before the next read, confirm CurrentWorkSection refreshes off the TicketChanged event — which it does — before removing, so behavior is preserved.)
confirmed.verdictReasoning:
I traced the actual code and the finding is real, but the severity is overstated.

REAL: TicketDetectionPresenter.kt:64-69 onAccept writes settings.state.activeTicketId/activeTicketSummary inline (lines 65-66) then calls ActiveTicketService.getInstance(project).setActiveTicket(...) (lines 67-68). ActiveTicketService.setActiveTicket (jira/.../ActiveTicketService.kt:77-83) launches WorkflowContextService.setActiveTicket on a background scope, which writes those EXACT same fields at WorkflowContextService.kt:341-342 (explicitly 'before any suspend point'). So the two inline assignments are a genuine duplicate of what the facade already persists.

The finder's claim about the sibling path is verified: SprintDashboardPanel.kt:265-273 onAccept was already cleaned up with the comment 'Phase 5 T13: facade now persists via WorkflowContextService and re-emits the legacy TicketChanged event itself, so the inline settings writes + EventBus emit are gone.' A repo-wide grep confirms TicketDetectionPresenter:65-66 are the ONLY remaining inline activeTicket settings writes in :jira — every other caller (SprintDashboardPanel:268/1134, JiraSearchContributorFactory:61, BranchingService:158/241) calls setActiveTicket(...) alone. This is the lone straggler.

NOT HARMFUL TO FIX: I checked the synchronous-read concern. (1) The synchronous setActiveTicket->read contract is satisfied by ActiveTicketService._localFlow (the in-memory cache updated synchronously at ActiveTicketService.kt:71), NOT by settings.state — proven by ActiveTicketServiceFacadeTest.kt:35-44, which asserts facade.activeTicketId synchronously and only verifies the canonical write/event after advanceUntilIdle(). (2) The onAccept closure itself does not read settings.state.activeTicketId after writing — it only logs (line 69). (3) CurrentWorkSection.refresh() (CurrentWorkSection.kt:73-84) reads settings.state.activeTicketId, but it is event-driven off TicketChanged (lines 65-66); in ActiveTicketService.kt:77-83 the canonical settings write (step 1) runs in the same coroutine BEFORE the TicketChanged emit (step 2), so the field is always fresh by the time refresh() reads it. (4) All other readers (PostCommitTransitionHandlerFactory:41, TimeTrackingCheckinHandlerFactory:46/102, WorkflowToolWindowFactory:143+, CreatePrPrefetch:179, BranchChangeTicketDetector:71) fire on later, separate user actions, never synchronously chained to onAccept. No test references TicketDetectionPresenter or TicketDetectionPopup; the two :core tests that set activeTicketId are WorkflowContextService fixtures, not pins on this presenter. Removal cannot break a passing test or a load-bearing read.

SEVERITY: Downgraded Medium -> Low. There is zero functional impact: the redundant write sets the identical values the facade will set, so leaving it in is harmless and removing it changes no behavior. It is pure dead/duplicated code (maintainability/consistency), not an edge-case with real impact. A clean, safe deletion, but the honest severity is Low/style.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Apply the fix: drop the two inline settings.state.activeTicket* assignments at TicketDetectionPresenter.kt:65-66, keeping only the setActiveTicket(...) call (mirroring SprintDashboardPanel:265-273). Downgrade severity to Low.

confirmed.title: WorklogSection bypasses the JiraService ToolResult contract and reaches into the raw JiraApiClient
confirmed.description: WorklogSection (a Sprint-tab ticket-detail UI component) obtains the low-level HTTP client via JiraServiceImpl.getApiClient() and calls apiClient.getWorklogs(issueKey) directly, consuming the internal ApiResult<JiraWorklogResponse> DTO. The core JiraService interface already exposes `getWorklogs(issueKey): ToolResult<List<WorklogData>>` (core/services/JiraService.kt:96) backed by the core model WorklogData. The sibling detail sections (DevStatusSection, ChangelogSection, LinkedDocsSection) all correctly go through project.getService(JiraService) and the ToolResult facade; WorklogSection is the lone outlier. This couples the UI to the raw transport/DTO layer instead of the core-model contract, duplicates response mapping, and means any agent/UI parity (LLM-optimized .summary, WorklogData model) is skipped for worklogs.
confirmed.evidence: val apiClient = jiraServiceImpl.getApiClient() ... val result = apiClient.getWorklogs(issueKey)  // returns ApiResult<JiraWorklogResponse>, not ToolResult<List<WorklogData>>
confirmed.suggestedFix: Replace the getApiClient()/apiClient.getWorklogs() path with project.getService(JiraService::class.java).getWorklogs(issueKey), branch on ToolResult.isError, and render from List<WorklogData> (mirroring ChangelogSection.loadHistory / DevStatusSection.loadDevStatus). This removes the raw-client dependency from the UI and reuses the existing core contract.
confirmed.verdictReasoning:
I independently confirmed every structural claim by reading the cited code.

CONFIRMED REAL: WorklogSection.kt:62 calls `jiraServiceImpl.getApiClient()` and line 71 calls `apiClient.getWorklogs(issueKey)`, consuming the raw `ApiResult<JiraWorklogResponse>` and rendering from `List<com.workflow.orchestrator.jira.api.dto.JiraWorklog>` (renderWorklogs, line 102) — the transport DTO, not the core model. The core JiraService interface DOES expose `getWorklogs(issueKey): ToolResult<List<WorklogData>>` (JiraService.kt:96) and JiraServiceImpl.getWorklogs (line 450) is fully implemented with field mapping + a meaningful .summary. The three sibling sections are the correct pattern: ChangelogSection.kt:59 (getTicketHistory), DevStatusSection.kt:61 (getFullDevStatus), LinkedDocsSection.kt:64 all go through project.getService(JiraService::class.java) + ToolResult. WorklogSection is genuinely the lone outlier, violating the CLAUDE.md core-interface->ToolResult facade convention and duplicating the DTO->display mapping.

FIX IS NOT HARMFUL: WorklogData (JiraModels.kt:166) preserves every field the UI consumes — author (pre-flattened to `displayName ?: "Unknown"`, matching the UI's own `?: "Unknown"`), timeSpentSeconds, comment, started. The only DTO field dropped (`created`) is unused by the UI. The internal helpers formatTimeSpent(Long)/formatStartedDate(String) operate on types both models expose identically, so they survive untouched. The null-apiClient branch maps to the service's isError path; the only delta is the "Jira not configured." message would collapse into "Could not load worklogs." — cosmetic wording, recoverable if the fix preserves a hint check. No test exercises WorklogSection rendering directly (the two worklog tests target the API client and service impl, which the fix does not touch), so nothing passing would break.

SEVERITY DOWNGRADE Medium->Low: There is no user-facing wrong behavior, no crash, no data loss. The agent already reaches worklogs through the core JiraService regardless of the UI's path, so agent/UI parity for the LLM .summary is not actually lost to any caller — it's just that the UI redundantly bypasses it. The concrete impact is duplicated DTO mapping and raw-transport coupling in one UI file: a real but minor maintainability deviation, best classified Low (style/consistency) rather than Medium.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Apply the suggested fix: route WorklogSection through project.getService(JiraService::class.java).getWorklogs(issueKey), branch on ToolResult.isError, and render from List<WorklogData> (mirroring ChangelogSection.loadHistory). Low-priority cleanup, not urgent.

confirmed.title: TicketDetectionPresenter writes activeTicket settings directly, duplicating ActiveTicketService's canonical persistence
confirmed.description: In the popup's onAccept handler the presenter writes settings.state.activeTicketId / activeTicketSummary inline AND then calls ActiveTicketService.setActiveTicket(...). ActiveTicketService is documented as the facade that owns the canonical write (it dispatches to WorkflowContextService.setActiveTicket and re-emits the legacy TicketChanged event). The parallel inline-write path was explicitly removed elsewhere for this exact reason — see SprintDashboardPanel.kt:266-267 ('facade now persists via WorkflowContextService ... so the inline settings writes + EventBus emit are gone'). Keeping the duplicate write here means two writers of the same persisted field with no ordering guarantee against the facade's async background write to WorkflowContextService, risking the project-local PluginSettings.state value and the canonical WorkflowContextService state diverging (e.g. if the canonical service later normalizes/clears).
confirmed.evidence:
onAccept = {
                    settings.state.activeTicketId = event.ticketKey
                    settings.state.activeTicketSummary = event.ticketSummary
                    ActiveTicketService.getInstance(project)
                        .setActiveTicket(event.ticketKey, event.ticketSummary)
confirmed.suggestedFix: Drop the two inline settings.state writes and call only ActiveTicketService.getInstance(project).setActiveTicket(event.ticketKey, event.ticketSummary), matching the cleanup already applied in SprintDashboardPanel.setupDetectionBanner (line 266-271). The facade is the single source of truth for activeTicket persistence.
confirmed.verdictReasoning:
STRUCTURAL CLAIM CONFIRMED: TicketDetectionPresenter.kt:65-66 does write settings.state.activeTicketId/activeTicketSummary inline, then line 67-68 calls ActiveTicketService.setActiveTicket(event.ticketKey, event.ticketSummary). That facade (ActiveTicketService.kt:77-83) launches WorkflowContextService.setActiveTicket(TicketRef(...)), which itself persists the same two settings fields (WorkflowContextService.kt:341-342, before any suspend point). So there are genuinely two writers of the same persisted field, and the parallel site SprintDashboardPanel.setupDetectionBanner WAS explicitly cleaned up (lines 266-271, comment 'facade now persists ... so the inline settings writes + EventBus emit are gone'). Git history confirms this is a missed cleanup: the presenter was created 2026-04-24 (eef3c441f) with the inline writes matching the then-current pattern, and the Phase 5 T13 facade conversion 2026-04-26 (05c4fb826) removed the inline writes from SprintDashboardPanel but missed the presenter. The presenter is the ONLY one of six setActiveTicket call sites in :jira that still does an inline settings write (the other five — SprintDashboardPanel:268/:1134, JiraSearchContributorFactory:61, BranchingService:158/:241 — call setActiveTicket only).

CLAIMED IMPACT REFUTED: The finder asserts a divergence/data-loss risk ('two writers ... no ordering guarantee ... risking PluginSettings and WorkflowContextService state diverging if the canonical service later normalizes/clears'). This impact does NOT exist in the current code: (1) both writers write the IDENTICAL literal strings event.ticketKey / event.ticketSummary; (2) WorkflowContextService.setActiveTicket performs NO normalization and NO clearing — line 341-342 is just '= ticket?.key.orEmpty()' on a non-null event key; (3) PluginSettings.activeTicketId is a plain BaseState 'by string("")' delegate (PluginSettings.kt:33-34) with no custom setter; (4) the facade's re-emitted TicketChanged is no-op'd by WorkflowEventMirror.handleTicketChanged's equality guard (WorkflowEventMirror.kt:64 'if state.activeTicket == incoming return'). Because there is exactly one possible final value regardless of write ordering, the two persisted views cannot diverge. The 'data-loss/ordering hazard' is therefore not present; this is pure redundant code. That puts honest severity at Low (latent maintainability — would only matter IF the canonical write ever began normalizing), not the Medium claimed.

FIX IS SAFE: Dropping lines 65-66 preserves the synchronous set-then-read contract (the facade sets _localFlow.value synchronously at ActiveTicketService.kt:71) and still persists settings via WorkflowContextService.setActiveTicket(341-342) before any suspend point. No test asserts the inline write — ActiveTicketServiceFacadeTest only verifies facade delegation (cache sync + coVerify on canonical.setActiveTicket + eventBus.emit). The fix matches the proven SprintDashboardPanel pattern. isReal=true because the duplicate write genuinely exists and the suggested fix is the correct cleanup; but severity is reassessed Low because the described harmful impact (divergence/data-loss) is impossible in the current code.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Apply the suggested cleanup (drop the two inline settings.state writes at TicketDetectionPresenter.kt:65-66, keep only the setActiveTicket call), but downgrade to Low — it is dead-redundant code, not a divergence/data-loss risk.

confirmed.title: QuickCommentPanel uses raw Swing JButton/JComboBox instead of JetBrains components
confirmed.description: The convention is 'JetBrains components only' for UI. QuickCommentPanel (pinned to the bottom of the Sprint-tab ticket detail) declares `private val sendButton = JButton(...)` and `private val visibilityCombo = JComboBox(visibilityModel)`. The codebase has JB equivalents in use elsewhere (e.g. com.intellij.openapi.ui.ComboBox, used in SprintDashboardPanel.sprintSelector and StartWorkDialog). Raw javax.swing.JComboBox does not pick up JB look-and-feel niceties (it works, hence Low). The text field already correctly uses JBTextField.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Replace javax.swing.JComboBox with com.intellij.openapi.ui.ComboBox (already imported across the module) for visibilityCombo. JButton has no direct JB replacement and is acceptable; leaving it is fine, but the combo should use the platform ComboBox for theme consistency.
confirmed.verdictReasoning:
I confirmed the literal code claims but the finding's rationale is overstated and its supporting evidence is self-contradicting.

WHAT IS TRUE:
- QuickCommentPanel.kt:42 declares `private val sendButton = JButton(AllIcons.Actions.Execute)` (raw javax.swing.JButton, imported line 26).
- QuickCommentPanel.kt:55 declares `private val visibilityCombo = JComboBox(visibilityModel)` (raw javax.swing.JComboBox, imported line 27).
- CLAUDE.md UX section does state "JetBrains components only".
- The text field correctly uses JBTextField (line 41).

WHY THE FINDING IS WEAK / PARTLY WRONG:
1. The cited counter-example is itself a counterexample-against-the-finder. StartWorkDialog is presented as "JB equivalents in use", but StartWorkDialog uses com.intellij.openapi.ui.ComboBox only for repoCombo (line 175) while using RAW javax.swing.JComboBox for existingBranchCombo (line 238) and sourceBranchCombo (line 296). PrDashboardPanel.kt:85 also uses raw `JComboBox(repoFilterModel)`. So raw JComboBox is a widespread pattern, not an isolated deviation in QuickCommentPanel.
2. The core justification — "Raw javax.swing.JComboBox does not pick up JB look-and-feel niceties" — is largely incorrect. Under the IntelliJ/Darcula LAF the platform installs its ComboBoxUI delegate on plain JComboBox via UIManager; the closed-combo and popup render with the platform look. com.intellij.openapi.ui.ComboBox mainly adds programmatic conveniences, not the theme itself. The finder itself concedes "it works, hence Low".
3. No test references QuickCommentPanel (grep found only QuickCommentPanel.kt and TicketDetailPanel.kt), so nothing pins the component type.

isReal = true only in the narrowest literal sense (a raw Swing component exists against a documented style convention), but the impact described (visual inconsistency) is overstated and the evidence (StartWorkDialog as a clean example) is false. This is a debatable style nit, correctly Low.

isHarmfulToFix = false. com.intellij.openapi.ui.ComboBox<E> extends javax.swing.JComboBox<E>, so swapping `JComboBox(visibilityModel)` -> `ComboBox(visibilityModel)` keeps every call site valid: bindBoundedWidth(ComboBoxWidth.DEFAULT) is defined on JComboBox<T> (ComboBoxes.kt:66) and applies to the subclass; `selectedItem as? VisibilityEntry` (line 155), `isEnabled` (lines 100/159/167/194), and DefaultComboBoxModel all still compile and behave identically. The fix is mechanical and safe. Leaving JButton as-is is also fine (no direct JB replacement; AllIcons icon button is the established pattern, mirrored in TicketDetectionPopup).

Severity: Low (style/consistency), consistent with the finder's own rating — arguably even trivial given the overstated rationale and the widespread same-pattern usage elsewhere.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Optional cosmetic cleanup, not a defect. If pursued for consistency, swap only visibilityCombo to com.intellij.openapi.ui.ComboBox(visibilityModel) (API-compatible: extends JComboBox, so bindBoundedWidth/selectedItem/isEnabled all keep working). Leave JButton(AllIcons.Actions.Execute) as-is — it is the established icon-button pattern. Note the finding's rationale is overstated: raw JComboBox already inherits the platform LAF delegate, and the cited "clean example" StartWorkDialog itself uses raw JComboBox at lines 238/296, as does PrDashboardPanel:85. Lowest priority.

confirmed.title: Comment write paths (post/reply/inline) pass repoName=null while reads use the PR's repo — writes land in the primary repo
confirmed.description: CommentsViewModel is constructed with the PR's own `projectKey`/`repoSlug` and uses them for `listPrComments`/`resolvePrComment`/`reopenPrComment`. But `postGeneralComment` calls `service.addPrComment(prId, text)` and `reply` calls `service.replyToComment(prId, parentCommentId, text)` — both leave `repoName` at its default `null`. In BitbucketServiceImpl, `addPrComment`/`replyToComment`/`addInlineComment` resolve the repo via `resolveRepo(repoName)`, and `repoName=null` falls back to the PRIMARY repo (resolveRepo → getPrimaryRepo). So in a multi-repo project, comments are LISTED from the correct repo but POSTED/REPLIED to a different (primary) repo's PR with the same id. AiReviewViewModel.pushFinding (AiReviewViewModel.kt:62-93) has the identical defect — it passes `repoName = null` to `addInlineComment`/`addPrComment` while listing findings against the PR's own coords. The AiReviewViewModelTest even asserts `repoName = null`, pinning the buggy behavior.
confirmed.evidence:
suspend fun postGeneralComment(text: String): Boolean {
    val result = service.addPrComment(prId = prId, text = text)   // repoName defaults to null -> primary repo
confirmed.suggestedFix: BitbucketService's comment write methods take `repoName` (a display name) but the ViewModels only have `projectKey`/`repoSlug`. Either add a projectKey/repoSlug overload for the write methods (consistent with listPrComments) and pass the PR's coords, or resolve the display `repoName` from the coords and pass it through. Then update the tests to assert the PR's repo is targeted rather than null.
confirmed.verdictReasoning:
I could not refute this finding; it is genuinely real and confirmed end-to-end.

WRITE PATHS pass repoName=null:
- CommentsViewModel.kt:82 postGeneralComment -> service.addPrComment(prId, text) (repoName defaults null); :93-97 reply -> service.replyToComment(prId, parentId, text) (null). Meanwhile :51-57 refresh, :108-113 resolve, :124-129 reopen all pass the PR's projectKey/repoSlug.
- AiReviewViewModel.kt:70-79 addInlineComment(..., repoName = null, ...) and :81 addPrComment(prId, finding.message, repoName = null), while :49 refresh lists against "$projectKey/$repoSlug/PR-$prId". Identical defect to CommentsViewModel, as the finding states.

FALLBACK TO PRIMARY REPO confirmed:
- BitbucketServiceImpl.kt:43-52 resolveRepo(repoName): when repoName==null it skips getRepoByName and falls to settings.getPrimaryRepo(). PluginSettings.kt:468 getPrimaryRepo() = repos.find{isPrimary} ?: firstOrNull(). All three write impls go through it: addPrComment :797, replyToComment :236, addInlineComment :211. So writes with null land in the PRIMARY repo, not the PR's repo.

MULTI-REPO IS REAL AND THE PR COORDS ARE THE INTENDED TARGET:
- RepoConfig (core) is a list with one isPrimary; RepositoriesConfigurable is a full settings UI; core CLAUDE.md documents cross-repo dashboard PR listing (getDashboardPullRequests). PrDetailPanel.showPr :334-335 / showPrDetail :412-413 thread the PR's own projectKey/repoSlug into currentPrProjectKey/currentPrRepoSlug, and PrDetailPanel.kt:824-828 explicitly comments "Use the PR's OWN coordinates ... so the review tab talks to the correct repo's Bitbucket." Those coords flow into CommentsTabPanel/CommentsViewModel (:971-977) and AiReviewTabPanel/AiReviewViewModel (:839-845). So when the focused PR belongs to a NON-primary repo, comments are LISTED from the right repo but POSTED/REPLIED to the primary repo's PR with the same id -> wrong-repo comment or a not-found error.

API ASYMMETRY (supports the fix as architecturally correct): reads (listPrComments/resolvePrComment/reopenPrComment/getPrComment) take explicit projectKey/repoSlug; the three write methods (BitbucketService.kt:35,48,132) only take a display repoName, resolved via getRepoByName -> RepoConfig.name (RepoConfig.kt:6/21-22), which the ViewModels do not hold. There is no projectKey/repoSlug overload for the writes. So the ViewModels literally cannot target the PR's repo today.

TESTS PIN THE BUG: AiReviewViewModelTest.kt:47,54,64,71,81-84,92-96 assert repoName=null; CommentsViewModelTest.kt:57,65,84 assert addPrComment(1,"hello",null)/replyToComment(1,50,"ack",null) while listing with "P","R". I ran both classes: BUILD SUCCESSFUL — they pass against the buggy behavior, exactly as the finding claims.

isHarmfulToFix = FALSE: single-repo (the common path) is unaffected because primary==PR repo. The fix only corrects multi-repo non-primary misrouting and does not remove the resolveRepo primary fallback (still needed for agent callers that legitimately pass null). Updating the two tests is correct since they pin buggy behavior, not load-bearing correctness.

SEVERITY: reassessed High -> Medium. It is a genuine wrong-behavior bug, but it only manifests in multi-repo projects when the focused PR is NOT in the primary repo (a real but conditional precondition). Most users on single-repo configs never hit it. Per the project's severity rubric this is an "edge-case with real impact" = Medium. High is defensible if multi-repo is the norm for this user base, but Medium is the more honest assessment given the gating precondition.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add projectKey/repoSlug overloads (or pass-through) for addPrComment / replyToComment / addInlineComment in BitbucketService, mirroring listPrComments, and have CommentsViewModel + AiReviewViewModel pass the PR's own coords. Update AiReviewViewModelTest and CommentsViewModelTest to assert the PR's repo is targeted instead of null.

confirmed.title: AiReviewTabPanel resolves toHash via getPullRequestCommits(prId, repoName=null) — wrong repo, mis-anchored inline comments
confirmed.description: AiReviewTabPanel is constructed with the PR's own `projectKey`/`repoSlug`, but to compute `toHash` (the commit used to pin inline review comments) it calls `service.getPullRequestCommits(prId, repoName = null)`. With `repoName=null`, BitbucketServiceImpl.getPullRequestCommits resolves to the PRIMARY repo. In a multi-repo project this fetches the commits of a DIFFERENT repo's PR #prId, yielding either an unrelated commit hash or empty. The resulting `toHash` is then used by AiReviewViewModel.pushFinding to pin inline comments with diffType=COMMIT — anchoring AI findings to the wrong commit (or losing the pin entirely). Same root cause as the comment-write mismatch.
confirmed.evidence:
val commits = service.getPullRequestCommits(prId, repoName = null)
if (commits.isError) "" else commits.data!!.firstOrNull()?.id.orEmpty()
confirmed.suggestedFix: Pass the panel's `projectKey`/`repoSlug` to a repo-aware commits lookup (resolve the display repoName from those coords, or add a coords overload) so toHash comes from the correct repo's PR head commit.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: I attempted to refute the finding by tracing the actual code; it holds up. CONFIRMED CHAIN: (1) AiReviewTabPanel.kt:82-86 calls service.getPullRequestCommits(prId, repoName = null) to compute toHash. (2) BitbucketServiceImpl.getPullRequestCommits -> resolveRepo(null) (lines 43-52) falls through to getPrimaryRepo() (PluginSettings.kt:468) — so repoName=null always targets the PRIMARY repo, never the panel's own repo. (3) AiReviewViewModel.pushFinding (lines 62-93) uses that toHash to pin inline comments with diffType=COMMIT (lines 69-78). The PR's own coords (projectKey/repoSlug) are held by the panel (AiReviewTabPanel.kt:27-28) and the ViewModel (lines 26-27) but never used for the service call — getPullRequestCommits/addInlineComment only accept repoName (BitbucketService.kt:24,35-45), matched against RepoConfig.name via getRepoByName (PluginSettings.kt:472), which is distinct from the Bitbucket coords (RepoConfig.kt:6-8,22). MULTI-REPO IS A REAL, INTENDED SCENARIO: PrDashboardPanel.kt:234-245 deliberately resolves the PR's own coords from its repoName badge ('critical for ... per-PR actions that would otherwise fall back to the project-default repo and open the wrong module's PR URL') and threads them via showPrDetail; PrDetailPanel.rebuildAiReviewTab:824-845 explicitly builds the panel with 'the PR's OWN coordinates' for exactly this reason. So when a user opens a non-primary repo's PR from the cross-repo dashboard, toHash is fetched from PRIMARY repo's PR #prId — an unrelated commit or empty. The finding's note 'Same root cause as the comment-write mismatch' is also verified: AiReviewViewModel.kt:76 and :81 pass repoName=null too, and runAiReview (PrDetailPanel.kt:866-867) fetches diff/changes with no repoName — so the entire AI-review flow operates on the primary repo. ADVERSARIAL CHECKS that did NOT rescue it: no coords overload exists; no caller pre-resolves repoName for these calls; the only test (AiReviewViewModelTest) asserts repoName=null but it is mock-based and merely pins current (buggy) behavior, not a correctness guarantee. isHarmfulToFix=false: the repoName=null->primary fallback is the wrong-repo fallback the rest of the codebase intentionally avoids; nothing load-bearing is removed (single-repo projects still resolve to the one configured/primary repo correctly via resolveRepo). Caveat: a narrow toHash-only fix would be incoherent (correct hash posted to wrong repo) — the fix must cover the whole flow. Severity Medium is accurate: harmless in single-repo (common case), but in multi-repo + non-primary + AI-review-push it mis-anchors findings or posts comments to a different repo's PR (a data-correctness issue bordering High, but gated behind several conditions).
confirmed.recommendedAction: Fix the whole AI-review flow to be repo-aware, not just toHash. Add a coords-based overload (projectKey/repoSlug) to BitbucketService.getPullRequestCommits / addInlineComment / addPrComment (and getPullRequestDiff/getPullRequestChanges used in runAiReview), OR resolve the display repoName from coords once and thread it through. AiReviewTabPanel:84, AiReviewViewModel:76/81, and PrDetailPanel.runAiReview:866-867 must all target the panel's projectKey/repoSlug instead of repoName=null. Update AiReviewViewModelTest (lines 64/71/82/94) to assert the resolved repo target rather than null.

confirmed.title: PrReviewSessionRegistry.get() does blocking file IO on the EDT during PR detail rendering
confirmed.description: AiReviewTabPanel's constructor calls `bindSessionIfExists()`, which synchronously calls `registry?.get(prKey)`. `PrReviewSessionRegistry.get` (PrReviewSessionRegistry.kt:40,55-61) acquires a ReentrantLock and reads the JSON registry file from disk (`Files.exists` + `Files.readString`). AiReviewTabPanel is constructed inside `PrDetailPanel.rebuildAiReviewTab`, which is invoked from `invokeLater {}` blocks in `showPr`/`showPrDetail` — i.e. on the EDT. Therefore disk IO (and lock contention with the background register/updateStatus writers) runs on the EDT every time a PR is selected. Although the file is small, this violates the project's threading convention (file/IO off the EDT) and is on a hot UI path that fires on every PR row click; under a slow/contended filesystem or a large registry it can stutter the UI.
confirmed.suggestedFix: In bindSessionIfExists, move the `registry.get(prKey)` read onto the existing IO `scope.launch` (the method already launches a coroutine for the toHash fetch) and only touch Swing inside `invokeLater`. Alternatively make the registry expose a suspend `get`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning:
Independently confirmed the full call chain. PrReviewSessionRegistry.get() (PrReviewSessionRegistry.kt:40) executes `lock.withLock { readFile() }`, and readFile() (lines 55-61) does `Files.exists(path)` + `Files.readString(path)` — genuine blocking file IO under a ReentrantLock that is shared with background register/updateStatus writers (lines 27-38). AiReviewTabPanel.init (line 59) calls bindSessionIfExists(), which calls `registry?.get(prKey)` synchronously (line 64). AiReviewTabPanel is constructed synchronously in PrDetailPanel.rebuildAiReviewTab (line 839), which is invoked inside invokeLater {} blocks in showPr (line 352 -> 360) and showPrDetail (line 416 -> 421), i.e. on the EDT. A second EDT entry exists via onSessionChanged() (line 943) inside invokeLater (line 924). So disk IO + lock acquisition runs on the EDT on every PR selection — a direct violation of the project's threading convention ('Blocking network/IO on the EDT is a freeze bug'). No readAction/ThreadingAssertions guard exists on this path (grep confirmed). The finding is REAL.

Severity Medium is honest: the registry file is a tiny Map<String, Entry> of {sessionId, status}, so the read is normally sub-millisecond and imperceptible. The real exposure is edge-case — slow/contended filesystem or lock contention with the concurrent background register() (called from runAiReview scope.launch, line 919) / updateStatus() writers. Bounded, usually-invisible cost on a hot path: convention violation with edge-case impact = Medium, not High (no wrong behavior in normal use).

isHarmfulToFix is FALSE. The suggested fix only changes AiReviewTabPanel.bindSessionIfExists: move registry.get() into the already-present scope.launch on Dispatchers.IO (line 82) and wrap the null/empty-state Swing mutations (statusLabel/listModel, lines 65-69) in invokeLater. The PrReviewSessionRegistryTest tests are pure persistence unit tests (register/get/updateStatus round-trips) with no threading assertions and do not touch the panel, so they remain green. No load-bearing fallback is removed. The fix is safe and architecturally consistent with the rest of the file (the toHash fetch already uses this exact pattern). Could not refute the finding.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Apply the suggested fix: in AiReviewTabPanel.bindSessionIfExists (pullrequest/.../ui/AiReviewTabPanel.kt:62-69) move the registry?.get(prKey) read onto the existing scope.launch (Dispatchers.IO, line 82) and wrap the null/empty-state Swing mutations (statusLabel/listModel) in invokeLater. The registry itself and PrReviewSessionRegistryTest need no changes. Optionally also route onSessionChanged() (PrDetailPanel.kt:943) through the same off-EDT path since it re-enters bindSessionIfExists from invokeLater.

confirmed.title: TicketChipInput.handlePastedText non-local return on MAX_CHIPS skips renderChips()/fireChange() for already-added chips
confirmed.description: In handlePastedText, the `tokens.forEach { ... }` loop adds chips and starts async resolution. When the limit is hit, `if (chips.size >= MAX_CHIPS) return` is a NON-LOCAL return out of `handlePastedText` (Kotlin `return` inside a `forEach` lambda returns from the enclosing function). This skips the trailing `renderChips()` and `fireChange()`. Any chips added by earlier iterations of the loop are therefore appended to the `chips` model but NOT rendered into the panel and do NOT fire the onChange snapshot, so the UI shows stale chips and the dialog's ticket state (provenance, validation) is out of sync until the next render is triggered by some other interaction.
confirmed.evidence:
tokens.forEach { token ->
    ...
    if (chips.size >= MAX_CHIPS) return   // non-local return: skips renderChips()/fireChange() below
    chips.add(Chip(normalized, Chip.Status.PENDING))
    resolveChipAsync(normalized)
}
renderChips()
fireChange()
confirmed.suggestedFix: Use `return@forEach` to skip the over-limit token while continuing the loop, then let the trailing `renderChips()`/`fireChange()` run; or break out of the loop and still call renderChips()/fireChange() afterward. The single-add path (addKey/commitInput) already returns BEFORE adding, so only this paste path is affected.
confirmed.verdictReasoning:
CODE-CONFIRMED. In /Users/subhankarhalder/.../pullrequest/ui/TicketChipInput.kt:481-491, the `tokens.forEach { token -> ... }` lambda contains `if (chips.size >= MAX_CHIPS) return` at line 485. In Kotlin, a bare `return` inside a `forEach` lambda is a non-local return that exits the enclosing `handlePastedText` function, so the trailing `renderChips()` (489) and `fireChange()` (490) are indeed skipped. When a multi-token paste crosses the MAX_CHIPS boundary mid-loop, earlier iterations have already called `chips.add(...)` (486) — those chips are in the model but the panel is not re-rendered and the onChange snapshot does not fire at that point. So the finding is accurate as written.

The finder's contrast with the single-add paths is also correct: addKey (192) and commitInput (223) check `chips.size >= MAX_CHIPS` and return BEFORE adding, so no half-applied state — only the paste path is affected.

SEVERITY = Low (finder's claim upheld; arguably even lower). Two strong mitigations cap the impact: (1) the paste path always calls `resolveChipAsync(normalized)` for every added chip (487) and does NOT use the initialContexts fast path, so each added chip launches a real async Jira lookup whose completion callback runs `renderChips()` + `fireChange()` (TicketChipInput.kt:295-296). The skipped render/fire therefore self-heals after the first lookup completes — a single network round-trip. (2) The dialog's validation and submit read the LIVE chips model, not the snapshot: doValidate() at CreatePrDialog.kt:1013 calls `ticketChipInput.allValid()`, and submit reads allValid()/validPrimary()/allChips() (832/911/932/1049). The chips model IS updated by line 486, so ticket validation/provenance/submit are correct regardless of whether fireChange() fired. The only genuinely-stale surface is the rendered chip panel (missing newly-added chip + inputField.isEnabled not updated via updateInputEnabled at 314-322) during the sub-second window before async resolution. Trigger conditions are narrow: multi-token paste, count crossing exactly MAX_CHIPS mid-loop, and the over-limit token must itself be a valid non-duplicate key (otherwise return@forEach at 483/484 fires instead).

NOT HARMFUL TO FIX. `return@forEach` is the correct idiomatic fix; remaining tokens would all be over-limit and harmlessly skipped, and the trailing renderChips()/fireChange() would run once. No existing test depends on the non-local return — TicketChipInputTest test 5 (lines 168-184) exercises addKey over-limit, and test 4 (148-164) pastes only 3 tokens (under the limit), so neither covers the paste-over-limit-mid-loop case; the fix passes both.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Apply the suggested fix: change line 485 from `if (chips.size >= MAX_CHIPS) return` to `if (chips.size >= MAX_CHIPS) return@forEach`, leaving the trailing renderChips()/fireChange() to run. Safe, idiomatic, no test relies on the current behavior.

confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete the dead Create-PR subsystem from PrDetailPanel: remove `showCreateForm`, `buildCreatePanel`, `submitCreatePr`, `renderCreateReviewers`, `showCreateReviewerPopup`, the `CARD_CREATE` constant + its `add(createPanel, CARD_CREATE)` registration + the `buildCreatePanel()` call in init, and the 13 `create*`/`selectedReviewer*`/`createRepoConfig` fields. (1) showCreateForm has ZERO callers: repo-wide grep across .kt/.java/.xml returns only its own definition at PrDetailPanel.kt:466; no reflection (getDeclaredMethod/getMethod on create methods) and no "showCreateForm" string literal anywhere. confirmed.title: Dead public method CreatePrPrefetch.resolveDefaultReviewersForBranch() — never called, doc references a non-existent method

Its `project` parameter is already marked `@Suppress("UNUSED_PARAMETER")`, and its KDoc plus the cross-reference at line 356 claim "The dialog re-runs this on target-change via [reloadDefaultReviewersForBranch]" — but no method named `reloadDefaultReviewersForBranch` exists, and CreatePrDialog.onTargetChanged() (CreatePrDialog.kt:915) only updates the provenance label; it never reloads reviewers. confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete `resolveDefaultReviewersForBranch` (lines 95-132) and remove the stale `[reloadDefaultReviewersForBranch]` sentence from the comment at lines 353-358 (keep the rest describing prefetchOneRepo's per-branch reviewer resolution, which is real). confirmed.verdictReasoning: I attempted to refute this finding but independently confirmed every claim. (1) Dead method: grep across all file types (kt/java/xml/json, excluding .git) shows resolveDefaultReviewersForBranch only at its own definition (CreatePrPrefetch.kt:106) and its two internal log.warn lines (124, 129) — zero callers anywhere, including the :agent tool layer and any reflection/string lookup. As a member of an `object` it is public-by-default, so it is dead public API. (2) Non-existent doc reference: reloadDefaultReviewersForBranch appears ONLY in the comment at line 356; `git log -S "fun reloadDefaultReviewersForBranch" --all` returns zero commits, proving it was never defined in repo history. (4) Half-implemented feature confirmed: both the method and the comment were introduced together in commit 02db77247 ("PR 6 of write-ops fix plan"); the real per-(source,target) reviewer resolution that actually shipped lives inline in prefetchOneRepo (lines 359-377) calling client.getDefaultReviewersForBranch directly, making the standalone helper redundant. Deleting the helper removes no load-bearing fallback because the genuine fallback (empty list on error → dialog uses prefetched chips) is implemented in prefetchOneRepo, not in the dead helper. I reassessed severity from Medium to Low: this is purely dead code plus a misleading KDoc/comment cross-reference with no user-facing behavior, no crash, no wrong behavior, no security impact — a minor maintainability/cleanup issue.

confirmed.title: Dead AUTHOR/REVIEWER branches and unused `username` param in PrListService.fetchAllPages()
confirmed.description: After the R-SWAP dashboard refactor (lines 145-148), AUTHOR and REVIEWER buckets are fetched via `fetchDashboardPrs`, and `fetchAllPages` is now called from exactly one site (line 170) with `role = "ALL"` and `username = null`. The `when (role)` inside `fetchAllPages` (lines 249-253) therefore can never take the `"AUTHOR"` or `"REVIEWER"` branches, and the `username: String?` parameter (line 242) is always null — both are dead. The method has degenerated into a single-role (`getRepoPullRequests`) paginator.
confirmed.evidence:
val results = fetchAllPages(client, projectKey, repoSlug, null, "ALL")  // only caller
...
"AUTHOR" -> client.getMyPullRequests(projectKey, repoSlug, currentState, username, start, 25)
"REVIEWER" -> client.getReviewingPullRequests(projectKey, repoSlug, currentState, username, start, 25)
confirmed.suggestedFix: Simplify `fetchAllPages` to drop the dead `username` param and the dead role branches: rename it (e.g. `fetchAllRepoPrPages`), remove the `when (role)` switch and call `client.getRepoPullRequests(projectKey, repoSlug, currentState, start, 25)` directly, and drop the now-unused `username`/`role` params at the single call site (line 170).
confirmed.verdictReasoning:
I independently confirmed every claim by reading PrListService.kt, grepping all callers, checking the core client signatures, and reading the two tests that touch PrListService.

1) Single caller: grep shows fetchAllPages is called at exactly ONE site — line 170: fetchAllPages(client, projectKey, repoSlug, null, "ALL"). The username arg is the literal null and role is the literal "ALL". No other call site exists.

2) AUTHOR/REVIEWER now go through fetchDashboardPrs: lines 160-165 dispatch authorDeferred/reviewerDeferred to fetchDashboardPrs(..., "AUTHOR"/"REVIEWER", ...), which uses client.getDashboardPullRequests (line 210) — a separate paginator. This matches the documented R-SWAP refactor in pullrequest/CLAUDE.md and core/CLAUDE.md.

3) Dead branches: the when(role) at lines 249-253 has "AUTHOR" (250) and "REVIEWER" (251) arms that are unreachable because role is hard-coded "ALL" at the only caller, so only the else arm (252: getRepoPullRequests) ever runs. Genuinely dead.

4) Dead username param: username: String? (line 242) is consumed ONLY by the two dead arms (250-251). The live else arm calls getRepoPullRequests, whose core signature (BitbucketBranchClient.kt:1548) takes no username. So the param is always null and never read on the live path. Genuinely dead.

5) Not globally dead / fix correctly scoped: getMyPullRequests/getReviewingPullRequests remain live elsewhere via BitbucketServiceImpl (lines 478/516, the agent-tool path) and BitbucketPrTool. The suggested fix only deletes the dead branches/param INSIDE fetchAllPages and does not remove those core methods, so the agent path is unaffected.

Test impact: neither passing test breaks. PrListServiceEdtMutationTest is a source-text pin on the withContext(Dispatchers.EDT) bitbucketUsername assignment (lines 110-121) — that 'username' is the auto-detected Bitbucket account, a different variable from the fetchAllPages param; the test never references fetchAllPages/role/the param. OpenPrListerImplTest mocks allRepoPrs and never exercises fetchAllPages.

Severity: this is pure dead code with no behavioral/crash/security impact and no load-bearing fallback. It is a maintainability issue — the dead multi-role when actively misleads a reader into thinking fetchAllPages is multi-role when it has degenerated into a single-role repo paginator. Medium is defensible (real maintainability impact / misleading surface); Low would also be reasonable since there is zero runtime impact. The suggested fix is safe and correctly scoped — not harmful.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Apply the suggested fix as scoped: remove the dead AUTHOR/REVIEWER when-branches and the unused username param from PrListService.fetchAllPages, calling client.getRepoPullRequests directly. Optionally rename to fetchAllRepoPrPages. Do NOT touch the core BitbucketBranchClient.getMyPullRequests/getReviewingPullRequests methods — those remain live on the agent path.

confirmed.title: Computed-but-unused `username` value in PrListService.refresh()
confirmed.description: The `val username = cachedUsername ?: ... ?: run { client.getCurrentUsername(); ... }` block (lines 110-128) binds a result that is never read after assignment — the three fetch paths in refresh() use `fetchDashboardPrs` (no username arg) and `fetchAllPages(..., null, "ALL")`. The only meaningful effect of the block is its side-effects: warming `cachedUsername` and persisting `connSettings.bitbucketUsername` (which BitbucketServiceImpl.resolveCurrentUsername later reads). As written, the `val username =` binding plus the `?: connSettings.bitbucketUsername.takeIf {...}` fallback compute a value purely to discard it, which obscures intent and triggers an unused-variable smell.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Refactor the value-returning expression into a side-effect-only `private suspend fun ensureUsernameCached(client)` that only warms `cachedUsername` + persists `connSettings.bitbucketUsername`, and call it without binding a result. This preserves the username-persistence behavior BitbucketServiceImpl depends on while removing the dead binding and the dead `connSettings.bitbucketUsername.takeIf{}` fallback that returns into nothing.
confirmed.verdictReasoning:
VERDICT: structurally real, but overstated severity (Medium -> Low). I could not refute the core claim.

Confirmed dead binding (PrListService.kt:110-128): `val username = cachedUsername ?: connSettings.bitbucketUsername.takeIf{...} ?: run{...}`. After line 128, `username` is never read. The three fetch paths do NOT consume it: fetchDashboardPrs(client,"AUTHOR",...) (line 161) and ("REVIEWER",...) (line 164) take no username arg (dashboard endpoint), and the ALL bucket calls fetchAllPages(client, projectKey, repoSlug, null, "ALL") with an explicit null (line 170). The only `username` tokens after line 128 are fetchAllPages's OWN parameter (lines 242/250/251), passed null at the call site. So the binding is genuinely dead. Finder is correct.

NOT pure dead code: the third branch `run{...}` (lines 112-128) carries load-bearing SIDE EFFECTS — it warms `cachedUsername` (line 115) and persists `connSettings.bitbucketUsername` on the EDT (lines 119-121). BitbucketServiceImpl.resolveCurrentUsername (line 511) reads ConnectionSettings.bitbucketUsername as its PRIMARY source, so the persistence is real and must survive any refactor. The finder explicitly acknowledges this, which is why this is a maintainability smell, not removable dead code.

isHarmfulToFix=false but with caveats. The suggested ensureUsernameCached() extraction can preserve behavior IF it keeps two invariants: (1) the `cachedUsername ?: settings ?:` SHORT-CIRCUIT so the expensive getCurrentUsername() API call only fires when no cached/saved username exists (a naive helper that always calls the API would add a network round-trip per refresh = a regression); (2) the EXACT source text `connSettings.bitbucketUsername = result.data` inside `withContext(Dispatchers.EDT)` — PrListServiceEdtMutationTest.kt is a brittle SOURCE-TEXT contract test (audit finding pullrequest:F-3) asserting that literal string appears within 200 chars after `withContext(Dispatchers.EDT)`. A careless rename/restructure would fail that passing test. So the fix is safe only if done carefully; it is not inherently breaking.

SEVERITY: code is functionally correct as written (Kotlin `val x = expr` with discarded result compiles and runs side effects fine). No crash, no data loss, no wrong user-facing behavior, no edge-case failure — purely "obscures intent / unused-variable smell." That is Low, not Medium. Finder overstated it as Medium ("real impact"); there is no behavioral impact. Recommend keeping as a Low cleanup, fixed carefully to preserve the short-circuit and the EDT-assignment contract test.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Downgrade to Low and rename the binding to make intent explicit (e.g. ensureUsernameCached side-effect helper) while preserving the cachedUsername short-circuit AND the exact `connSettings.bitbucketUsername = result.data` text the contract test pins. Low priority; not a bug.

confirmed.title: Dead compat-shim parameter PrActionService.decline(cachedVersion) — never read, contradicts no-compat-shim convention
confirmed.description: `suspend fun decline(prId: Int, cachedVersion: Int = 0)` never reads `cachedVersion` — `fetchAndDecline` always GETs a fresh version (lines 249-263), and the KDoc states the param is "intentionally unused ... retained only for API compatibility." The sole caller (PrDetailPanel.kt:1263) and the pinned test (PrActionServiceDeclineRetryTest) both call `decline(prId)` without it. The same file explicitly cites `feedback_no_compat_shims.md` when it dropped the analogous `version` param from `merge`/`updateDescription` (lines 146-151, 300-306), so this lingering shim is inconsistent with the module's own convention.
confirmed.evidence:
suspend fun decline(prId: Int, cachedVersion: Int = 0): ApiResult<Unit> {
...
 * posting the decline request, so [cachedVersion] is intentionally unused
 * in the primary attempt — passing it is optional and retained only for API
 * compatibility.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Drop the `cachedVersion: Int = 0` parameter (signature → `decline(prId: Int)`) and trim the KDoc lines describing it, matching the no-compat-shim treatment already applied to merge/updateDescription. No caller or test passes it, so this is safe.
confirmed.verdictReasoning:
I independently confirmed every claim by reading PrActionService.kt:185-263, the sole caller, and the pinned test.

1) `cachedVersion` is genuinely dead. The signature at line 196 declares `decline(prId: Int, cachedVersion: Int = 0)`, but the function body (lines 196-243) references only `prId`. The actual version always comes from a fresh GET: `fetchAndDecline` (lines 249-263) calls `client.getPullRequestDetail(...)` (line 255) and posts decline with `current.version` (line 259). `cachedVersion` is never read anywhere in the body.

2) The KDoc self-incriminates: lines 190-191 state the param is "intentionally unused in the primary attempt — passing it is optional and retained only for API compatibility."

3) Repo-wide grep for `cachedVersion` returns only 3 hits, all in this file's KDoc (188, 190) and the signature (196). No caller anywhere passes it. The single production caller, PrDetailPanel.kt:1263, calls `decline(prId)` with no version. The pinned PrActionServiceDeclineRetryTest exercises the BitbucketBranchClient seam directly (`client.declinePullRequest`, `client.getPullRequestDetail`) and never invokes `PrActionService.decline()` at all — so it has zero dependency on the parameter.

4) The convention inconsistency is real and explicitly self-cited: the same file dropped the analogous `version` param from merge (KDoc line 150, "Per feedback_no_compat_shims.md the parameter is removed cleanly rather than ignored") and updateDescription (line 304, "The previous explicit version parameter is dropped (per feedback_no_compat_shims.md)"). The lingering `decline(cachedVersion)` is the lone exception that violates the module's own stated rule.

5) Fix is safe / not harmful. Because `cachedVersion` has a default value and is never bound by any caller, removing it changes no behavior: the sole caller already uses the `decline(prId)` form. No compile break, no test break (the test never touches it), and the 409 retry logic (lines 205-242) is wholly independent of the parameter. The fix is purely mechanical signature + KDoc cleanup.

Severity confirmed Low — it is a maintainability/style inconsistency (dead parameter that contradicts a documented convention), not wrong behavior, crash, or data loss. The param being dead means it cannot affect runtime correctness either way.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Apply the suggested fix: drop the `cachedVersion: Int = 0` parameter so the signature becomes `decline(prId: Int)`, and trim the KDoc lines (188-194) that describe the unused param. This matches the no-compat-shim treatment already applied to merge (line 150) and updateDescription (line 304).

confirmed.title: Redundant null-coalescing on non-null repoName in PrDashboardPanel
confirmed.description: `val repoName = prDetail.repoName ?: ""` applies an Elvis fallback to `BitbucketPrDetail.repoName`, which is declared `var repoName: String = ""` in core (BitbucketBranchClient.kt:340) — a non-null type. The `?: ""` can never fire and produces a Kotlin "unnecessary non-null assertion / Elvis on non-null" smell. Elsewhere in the same class repoName is used directly without the guard (e.g. line 473 `repoName = repoName`), confirming it is non-null.
confirmed.evidence: val repoName = prDetail.repoName ?: ""
confirmed.suggestedFix: Replace with `val repoName = prDetail.repoName` (drop the redundant `?: ""`).
confirmed.verdictReasoning:
Attempted to refute but the finding holds. Confirmed independently from source:

1. PrDashboardPanel.kt:253 reads `val repoName = prDetail.repoName ?: ""`. The import at line 15 (`com.workflow.orchestrator.core.bitbucket.BitbucketPrDetail`) ties `prDetail` to the core DTO.

2. The core DTO declares the field as non-null with a default: `BitbucketBranchClient.kt:338-340` — `@kotlinx.serialization.Transient var repoName: String = ""`. (The finder cited package path `core/services/BitbucketBranchClient.kt:340`; the real path is `core/bitbucket/BitbucketBranchClient.kt:340` — same line, same field, same type; minor path slip, not a substantive error.) Type is `String` (non-null), so `?: ""` can never fire — Kotlin emits the "Elvis always returns left operand of non-nullable type" warning. Dead branch.

3. Internal contradiction confirming non-null: same method, 15 lines earlier, line 238 does `prDetail.repoName.takeIf { it.isNotBlank() }` — a plain (non-safe-call) member access; this would not compile on a nullable receiver. Line 605 uses `it.repoName == primaryLabel` directly; lines 429-432 filter on `it.repoName` directly. All confirm non-null.

4. Downstream sinks are also non-null `String`: `PrRef.repoName` (Refs.kt:14) and `WorkflowEvent.PrSelected.repoName` (WorkflowEvent.kt:172), into which line 271/285 pass `repoName`.

isHarmfulToFix = false: replacing `prDetail.repoName ?: ""` with `prDetail.repoName` is behaviorally identical. The field's own default is already `""`, the type guarantees non-null, so the value passed downstream is unchanged. No load-bearing fallback is removed; no reachable code path or test can depend on the unreachable Elvis branch.

Severity stays Low: genuine but purely a dead-code / style smell with zero runtime impact — not wrong behavior users hit.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Apply the suggested fix: drop the redundant `?: ""` so line 253 reads `val repoName = prDetail.repoName`. Low priority; a clean style cleanup with no behavioral risk. Optionally fold into a broader cleanup pass rather than a standalone commit.

rejected.title: Saved-filter run in SprintDashboardPanel bypasses JiraService.searchTickets and calls the raw JiraApiClient
rejected.reason:
The literal code at SprintDashboardPanel.kt:711-718 matches the evidence: onFilterClicked() calls `service.getApiClient()` then `api.searchByJql(jql, 50)`, yielding `List<JiraIssue>`. So the bypass technically exists. BUT the finding mischaracterizes it as an isolated/intentional-coupling layering leak with real maintainability impact. Adversarial trace shows otherwise:

1. The ENTIRE Sprint dashboard subsystem is built on the internal `JiraIssue` DTO, not just this one method. The list model is `DefaultListModel<JiraIssue>` (line 84); the renderer is `TicketListCellRenderer : ListCellRenderer<JiraIssue>` (TicketListCellRenderer.kt:25); `allIssues`/`sprintIssuesSnapshot` are `List<JiraIssue>` (lines 173, 202); selection feeds `detailPanel.showIssue(issue: JiraIssue)` (TicketDetailPanel.kt:124); StartWorkAction feeds `branchingService.generateBranchName(selectedIssue: JiraIssue, ...)`; `sortIssues`/`isHeader`/`SprintTimeBar.updateFromIssues` all consume `JiraIssue`.

2. The PRIMARY sprint-load path (line 574-588) gets its `JiraIssue` list from `SprintService.loadSprintIssues(...)` which returns `ApiResult<List<JiraIssue>>` — i.e. the filter path is CONSISTENT with the panel's established design, not an aberrant one-off leak.

3. The renderer hard-depends on fields `JiraTicketData` does NOT carry compatibly: `issue.fields.status.statusCategory?.key` (border accent/pill color/strikethrough at lines 121,134,202; and sort at 805/811) — JiraTicketData.status is a flat String with no statusCategory; `issue.id.startsWith("header-")` for synthetic assignee-group section headers (renderer:55, panel:55/205/489/930/1222) — JiraTicketData has no `id` field at all; blocker count via `issue.fields.issuelinks[].type.inward`/`inwardIssue` (255-257) — JiraTicketData uses a differently-shaped `linkedIssues`.

4. Layering: this is :jira/ui reaching :jira's own :jira/api JiraApiClient + :jira DTO. JiraService/searchTickets is the CROSS-MODULE + agent contract (consumed by :core/:agent), not a mandate that an internal panel route its own rendering through the lossy core facade.

5. The suggested fix (route through searchTickets → JiraTicketData, or add a thin mapper used by both) is HARMFUL/impractical as written: JiraTicketData is lossy for this UI (no statusCategory, no id-based headers, incompatible link shape) and cannot feed showIssue(JiraIssue)/generateBranchName(JiraIssue). Doing it 'properly' would require rewriting the whole sprint dashboard rendering subsystem onto a new display model — orders of magnitude beyond a 'filter run' fix — and naively swapping in searchTickets would break status coloring, section headers, and blocker badges.

Tests: only SprintFavouritesRenderingTest exists, covering SavedFiltersSection.shouldShowSection visibility logic — NOT onFilterClicked/searchByJql. So there is no failing/contract test either way; the naive fix wouldn't fail a test but would visibly degrade rendering at runtime.

Verdict: the bypass is real as a literal code observation, but it is the deliberate, panel-wide architecture for a :jira-internal rendering path, not a cross-module contract violation and not a Medium-impact maintainability issue. Severity overstated; the recommended fix is the wrong move. Downgrade to Low (defensible-consistency / minor style observation), recommend WONTFIX as specified.

rejected.title: Merge action discards the ApiResult, swallowing merge failures and disabling buttons as if merge succeeded
rejected.reason:
I attempted to refute this finding but independently confirmed it from the code. The merge button handler in PrDetailPanel.kt:1223-1247 calls PrActionService.getInstance(project).merge(...) (lines 1227-1232) and discards the returned value. The merge() method at PrActionService.kt:153-183 returns ApiResult<Unit> and returns ApiResult.Error (NOT throws) on every normal failure path: not-configured (lines 160, 162) and merge failure after retry (line 178-181). The underlying BitbucketBranchClient.mergePullRequestWithRetry (BitbucketBranchClient.kt:2048-2095) returns ApiResult.Error with VALIDATION_ERROR for vetoes/conflicts and STALE_VERSION for unresolved 409s — these are typed errors, not thrown exceptions. Therefore lines 1233-1238 unconditionally disable mergeButton/approveButton/needsWorkButton/declineButton on any normal return, and the catch (e: Exception) at lines 1239-1247 only fires for genuine exceptions/cancellation, never for ApiResult.Error. Net effect on a failed merge: all action buttons greyed out (identical to success UI) and no error notification — user believes the PR merged when it is still open. This is wrong behavior users hit in normal use (vetoes, conflicts, permission errors are common), so High severity is correct.

The finding is corroborated by the three sibling mutation handlers in the same file that all correctly branch on the ApiResult: Approve (lines 1182-1193, when(result) Success/Error), Decline (lines 1263-1279, checks result.isError, notifies on error, disables buttons only on success), and Needs Work (lines 1296-1306, when(result) Success/Error). The merge handler is the lone outlier that ignores its result.

isHarmfulToFix is false: the suggested fix (capture result, branch Success→disable+refreshCurrentPr, Error→keep enabled + notify) uses methods that already exist in the panel (refreshCurrentPr at line 1584, showNotification at line 1599, both already used by the sibling handlers). No test exercises the merge button result handling — the only mergeButton test (HtmlEscapeInVetoLabelsTest.kt:57) covers tooltip text, not the action listener — so the fix breaks no passing test. The fix simply brings the merge handler into line with the established pattern in the same file.

rejected.title: PR detail body + all detail-panel actions ignore the selected PR's repo and hit the primary repo (multi-repo)
rejected.reason:
I tried to refute this and could not; every claim checks out against the code.

CONFIRMED — multi-repo is real and feeds non-primary PRs into the panel: PrListService.refresh() iterates `pluginSettings.getRepos().filter { it.isConfigured }` (PrListService.kt:131) and pulls PRs via the cross-repo `getDashboardPullRequests(...)` endpoint (line 210). PrDashboardPanel's onPrSelected resolves each PR's OWN repo from its repoName badge and threads it in: `detailPanel.showPrDetail(prDetail, projectKey = prRepoConfig?.bitbucketProjectKey, repoSlug = prRepoConfig?.bitbucketRepoSlug)` (PrDashboardPanel.kt:241-245). So a non-primary-repo PR can be selected.

CONFIRMED — the services are repo-blind: PrDetailService.kt:36-43 and PrActionService.kt:42-49 resolve projectKey()/repoSlug() SOLELY from `PluginSettings.state.bitbucketProjectKey`/`bitbucketRepoSlug` (the primary). None of the public methods (getDetail/getActivities/getChanges/getCommits/getDiff/checkMergeStatus, approve/unapprove/merge/decline/updateTitle/updateDescription/addReviewer/removeReviewer/setNeedsWork/addComment/replyToComment) take a repo parameter.

CONFIRMED — every PrDetailPanel call site passes only prId: lines 341/380/428/443/1168/1182/1227/1263/1296/1521/1564/1623/1980/2020/2122/2447/2489/2580/2717 all call `...getInstance(project).<method>(prId, ...)` with no coords. So body + all mutations hit the primary repo.

SMOKING GUN — the panel proves it knows better but only half-uses it: it threads currentPrProjectKey/currentPrRepoSlug into Open-in-Browser (1332-1337), the Comments tab (964-969), the AI Review tab (833-838), and even the add-reviewer USER SEARCH popup (1617-1618) — then immediately discards those coords when it calls `PrActionService.getInstance(project).addReviewer(prId, user.name)` (1623). The popup lists users from the correct repo, then the mutation lands on the primary repo.

SECOND SMOKING GUN — the codebase already fixed this exact bug on the agent path: BitbucketServiceImpl.addReviewer (lines 300-337) and updatePrTitle (343-358) detect `!isPrimaryRepo(projectKey, repoSlug)` and route around PrActionService via modifyPullRequestForAgent, with the comment (304-308): "PrActionService is single-repo (resolves via settings.bitbucketProjectKey/RepoSlug). When the agent targets a non-primary repo, route directly ... so we still get retry-on-409 without leaking settings-vs-tool repo selection through PrActionService." The UI panel has no equivalent guard.

NOT HARMFUL TO FIX: the existing tests (PrActionServiceModifyRetryTest, PrActionServiceMutatorsTest, PrActionServiceDeclineRetryTest) exercise `client.modifyPullRequest(RepoCoords("P","r"), ...)` and the pure mutators directly — they do not assert PrActionService's scalar-settings resolution and would not break if the methods gained optional projectKey/repoSlug params (default to scalar primary preserves single-repo back-compat). The suggested fix is exactly the pattern already shipping in BitbucketServiceImpl.

SEVERITY High (boundary with Critical): in the common case the wrong-repo query just 404s (no PR with that id in the primary repo) → wrong/empty data or a failed action. But Bitbucket PR ids are PER-REPO and every repo numbers from #1, so id collisions across configured repos are routine; when the primary repo has a PR with the same numeric id, merge/decline/updateTitle/updateDescription/addReviewer silently MUTATE the wrong PR in the primary repo — silent cross-PR data corruption. Rated High because it requires a multi-repo setup (and, for the corruption variant, an id collision), but the data-corruption potential is real and the broken-read variant is hit in normal multi-repo use.

rejected.title: renderPrHeader reads non-volatile currentPr from an IO coroutine — cross-thread visibility gap
rejected.reason:
REFUTED. The finding's central premise — "There is no happens-before edge between the EDT write of currentPr and this IO read, so the coroutine can observe a stale/older currentPr" — is factually wrong under the Java/Kotlin memory model.

Threading topology of currentPr vs cachedUsername is OPPOSITE, which is the crux:
- cachedUsername (PrDetailPanel.kt:1456-1465) is WRITTEN inside the IO coroutine (line 1462) and READ on a separate EDT path (per its KDoc, the renderReviewers approval check). Those two accesses are genuinely concurrent with no dispatch edge connecting them, so @Volatile is correctly required there (F-12 fix, commit 29d29a0c4).
- currentPr in the cited path is WRITTEN on the EDT and READ inside the coroutine that renderPrHeader itself launches. There IS a happens-before edge: kotlinx.coroutines `launch` dispatches the task onto Dispatchers.IO via a release into the dispatcher's task queue; the IO worker that runs the body performs the matching acquire, so everything that happened-before the `scope.launch` call on the launching thread is visible to the coroutine body.

Tracing both callers confirms the write happens-before the launch:
- showPr (line 352-355): on the EDT, `currentPr = prDetail` (line 354) runs in program order before `renderPrHeader(...)` (line 355), which calls `scope.launch` (line 1425). Same thread, program order → the launch dispatch carries the write's visibility. No stale read possible.
- showPrDetail (lines 411,416-417): caller thread writes `currentPr = pr` (line 411) then `invokeLater(...)` (line 416, a release into the EDT queue); the EDT acquires, runs renderPrHeader (line 417), then scope.launch. The chain write→invokeLater-enqueue→EDT-dequeue→launch-enqueue→IO-dequeue→read is a transitive happens-before chain. No stale read possible.

All other writes to currentPr (lines 323/333/411/467/592/1592) occur on the EDT (entry-point UI methods or inside invokeLater), so there is no concurrent EDT writer racing the coroutine's read in a way that produces the visibility gap claimed. The finder treated currentPr and cachedUsername as symmetric; they are not.

No tests exercise this logic (grep of pullrequest/src/test for currentUserApproved/resolveCurrentUsername/renderPrHeader returned nothing), so the suggested fix would not break a passing test, and marking a reference field @Volatile or snapshotting it on the EDT removes no fallback — hence isHarmfulToFix=false. But the fix is unnecessary because no visibility bug exists. Severity stays at most Low: at best a defensive-consistency/style nit (one could mark currentPr @Volatile to mirror cachedUsername), not a correctness defect.
